path.label: Literature & rhetoric
path.description: The 800s hold every work that the catalog considers belles-lettres — fiction, poetry, drama, criticism — across all languages and periods.

path.label: American literature in English
path.description: American literature is a division of 800 because Dewey was designed in 1876 by an American librarian whose categories reflected the holdings he was actually shelving.

path.label: American fiction in English
path.description: Within American literature, fiction sits at .3. Poetry is .1, drama is .2. The order is not arbitrary — Dewey ordered the genres by what he believed was their relative literary weight.

path.label: American fiction · 1900–
path.description: The .5 chronological subdivision was added in the 11th edition (1923) to separate post-1900 fiction from earlier centuries — a recognition that 'modern' fiction needed its own shelving.

path.label: American fiction · 1945–1999
path.description: Postwar through end-of-century. King's entire active publishing life sits here. The 21st-century portion of his career has begun to spill into 813.6.

headings.label: Horror tales
headings.note: The genre signal. FAST's topical facet groups works by what they are *about as form*.

headings.label: Maine
headings.note: The novel's setting. Geographic facets carry their own controlled vocabulary, rooted in LCSH place names but with FAST's flattened syntax.

headings.label: Children — Crimes against
headings.note: A topical heading describing what the book engages with — not what genre it is, but what its subject matter touches.

headings.label: Clowns
headings.note: FAST is granular enough that even a recurring symbol can earn its own access point if a book engages with it heavily.

headings.label: Fiction
headings.note: The form facet. Separated from the topical facet so a non-fiction book about horror is still findable distinctly from a horror novel.

headings.label: 20th century
headings.note: Chronological facets attach to subjects (when the work depicts) rather than to publication date (which lives in the catalog record proper).

fast.philosophy: A book in FAST is not in one place — it is at the intersection of all its access points. Two visitors looking for it will arrive from different directions: one looking for horror tales, one looking for Maine, one looking for novels about children. Each path is a real way the catalog can find the book.
